name: Temperature
description: Environmental heat, cold, wetness, shelter, and insulation affect player exposure and survival penalties.
design_notes: Temperature works when players can read the exposure equation: temperature_zone_id, clothing insulation, wetness_state, wind or shelter modifiers, and threshold effects. The mechanic should explain why the player is cold, hot, protected, or recovering before health loss begins.
design_purpose: Make biomes, weather, clothing, shelter, and campfires matter in route planning.
Create survival pressure that changes with location and preparation rather than only inventory stockpiles.
Encourage base building, crafting, and equipment choices through environmental risk.
player_fantasy: The player feels the environment itself is dangerous and learns to prepare for exposure.

parameters.name: temperature_zone_id
parameters.description: Environmental zone identifier that provides ambient exposure values and weather modifiers.

parameters.name: insulation_rating_points
parameters.description: Combined protection from clothing, armor, shelter, buffs, or temporary heat sources.

parameters.name: wetness_state_id
parameters.description: Moisture state that modifies cold exposure, drying behavior, and warning messages.

parameters.name: shelter_volume_id
parameters.description: Detected shelter area that reduces wind, rain, heat, or cold exposure.

parameters.name: exposure_threshold_id
parameters.description: Threshold table that maps exposure score into warnings, penalties, and recovery states.

parameters.name: safe_zone_id
parameters.description: Area or source that suspends or reverses exposure buildup while the player remains inside it.

edge_cases: The player crosses multiple temperature zones while inside a vehicle, cave, or player-built shelter.
Wetness from swimming or rain persists through fast travel, sleeping, or save/load restoration.
A heat source warms the player but does not protect food, water, or nearby crafting stations.
Multiplayer bases update shelter coverage while clients are standing inside partially built structures.
common_bugs: Exposure damage starts before UI warning thresholds appear because warning and penalty tables use different ids.
Shelter detection checks only roof collision and ignores open walls, wind direction, or structure ownership.
Insulation modifiers stack permanently when equipment is swapped quickly.
Wetness drying runs during pause or offline progress and removes intended survival pressure.
balancing_notes: Exposure should ramp through warning, penalty, and danger bands rather than jumping directly to health loss.
Harsh zones work best when players have at least one clear preparation path: clothing, shelter, food, heat, or route timing.
Temperature can enrich exploration, but constant micro-management will compete with hunger, thirst, and combat.
accessibility_notes: Do not rely only on screen tint or particle effects; show exposure reason, severity, and recovery source in readable UI.
Offer reduced exposure severity or longer warning buffers for players who need more planning time.

implementation_notes.unity: Compute exposure from temperature_zone_id, insulation_rating_points, wetness_state_id, and shelter_volume_id in a survival service. Use trigger volumes and structure queries for shelter, persist wetness and exposure stage, and keep UI warnings driven by the same threshold table as penalties.
implementation_notes.godot: Areas can provide temperature zones and shelter volumes, while a player survival node aggregates equipment and wetness modifiers. Save wetness, exposure accumulator, and active zone ids so reloading inside weather does not reset danger or duplicate penalties.
implementation_notes.unreal: Use volumes, Gameplay Tags, and replicated survival attributes for exposure state. Server authority should calculate zone, shelter, and equipment modifiers, then replicate warning bands to UMG so client presentation matches damage decisions.
implementation_notes.web: Keep exposure calculation in simulation state using zone ids and equipment metadata. Browser visibility changes should follow pause or offline policy; do not let wall-clock gaps silently freeze or over-advance exposure.

relationships.reason: Day and night phases can drive exposure changes that raise or lower temperature pressure.
Shelter volumes and base structures can provide insulation, safe zones, or recovery sources.
Extreme exposure can apply health penalties after cold or heat thresholds persist.

example_games.title: The Long Dark
example_games.note: Uses cold, clothing, shelter, and weather as major survival pressures.

example_games.title: The Legend of Zelda: Breath of the Wild
example_games.note: Uses hot and cold regions with equipment and consumable counterplay.
